channel.title: IT-Security & Privacy News Aggregator
channel.description: Aggregierter RSS-Feed aus führenden IT-Security, Privacy und Cyber Policy Quellen

item.title: Chipmaker Patch Tuesday: Intel and AMD Patch 70 Vulnerabilities
item.description: <p>The two chip giants have published over two dozen advisories describing recently identified security defects.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/chipmaker-patch-tuesday-intel-and-amd-patch-70-vulnerabilities/">Chipmaker Patch Tuesday: Intel and AMD Patch 70 Vulnerabilities</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Ionut Arghire

item.title: Microsoft Fixes 17 Critical Flaws in May Patch Tuesday
item.description: Microsoft has patched 120 vulnerabilities in this month’s security update round

item.title: Hundreds of Malicious Packages Force RubyGems to Suspend Registrations
item.description: <p>More than 500 packages were pushed during the attack, but the target appears to have been RubyGems itself rather than users.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/hundreds-of-malicious-packages-force-rubygems-to-suspend-registrations/">Hundreds of Malicious Packages Force RubyGems to Suspend Registrations</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Eduard Kovacs

item.title: Android Adds Intrusion Logging for Sophisticated Spyware Forensics
item.description: Google on Tuesday unveiled a new opt-in Android feature called Intrusion Logging for storing forensic logs to better analyze sophisticated spyware attacks. Intrusion Logging, available as part of Advanced Protection Mode, enables "persistent and privacy-preserving forensics logging to allow for investigation of devices in the event of a suspected compromise," the company said. The feature, it
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: ICS Patch Tuesday: New Security Advisories From Siemens, Schneider, CISA
item.description: <p>Many ICS vendors have not released new advisories for the May 2026 Patch Tuesday.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/ics-patch-tuesday-new-security-advisories-from-siemens-schneider-cisa/">ICS Patch Tuesday: New Security Advisories From Siemens, Schneider, CISA</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Eduard Kovacs

item.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
item.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.
item.dc:creator: BrianKrebs

item.title: It's Patch Tuesday for Microsoft and Not a Zero-Day In Sight
item.description: It's the first time in two years with no zero-days. But with 137 flaws to patch, including nine critical ones, admins still have plenty of work to do.
item.dc:creator: Jai Vijayan

item.title: Over 1 Million Baby Monitors, Security Cameras Exposed Through Meari Flaws
item.description: <p>Meari IoT flaws reportedly exposed baby monitor images, camera activity, and device data across more than 1 million connected devices.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-meari-iot-baby-monitor-camera-vulnerabilities/">Over 1 Million Baby Monitors, Security Cameras Exposed Through Meari Flaws</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Ken Underhill

item.title: Microsoft Patches 137 Vulnerabilities
item.description: <p>Fresh security updates resolve critical flaws in Azure, Windows, Dynamics 365, and the SSO Plugin for Jira &#038; Confluence.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/microsoft-patches-137-vulnerabilities/">Microsoft Patches 137 Vulnerabilities</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Ionut Arghire

item.title: Exaforce Raises $125 Million for Agentic SOC Platform
item.description: <p>Exaforce has raised a total of $200 million and plans on using the latest investment for product development and international expansion. </p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/exaforce-raises-125-million-for-agentic-soc-platform/">Exaforce Raises $125 Million for Agentic SOC Platform</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: SecurityWeek News

item.title: Adobe Patches 52 Vulnerabilities in 10 Products
item.description: <p>While none of the flaws have been exploited in the wild, many of them could lead to arbitrary code execution.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/adobe-patches-52-vulnerabilities-in-10-products/">Adobe Patches 52 Vulnerabilities in 10 Products</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Ionut Arghire

item.title: New Exim BDAT Vulnerability Exposes GnuTLS Builds to Potential Code Execution
item.description: Exim has released security updates to address a severe security issue affecting certain configurations that could enable memory corruption and potential code execution. Exim is an open-source Mail Transfer Agent (MTA) designed for Unix-like systems to receive, route, and deliver email. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-45185, aka Dead.Letter, has been described as a use-after-free
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: White Circle Raises $11 Million for AI Control Platform
item.description: <p>The startup will invest in accelerating product development, hiring new talent, and expanding its customer base.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/white-circle-raises-11-million-for-ai-control-platform/">White Circle Raises $11 Million for AI Control Platform</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Ionut Arghire

item.title: OpenAI Launches 'Daybreak' to Help Build Secure By Design Software
item.description: With Daybreak, OpenAI wants its frontier AI models to be used to deploy secure by design software from the ground up

item.title: AI GitHub Agents: How One Issue Leaked Private Repos | UpGuard
item.description: Uncover how indirect prompt injection and tool poisoning allow attackers to exfiltrate private data via AI agents—all without stealing a single credential.

item.title: 1 in 15 MCP Servers are Lookalikes: Is Your Org at Risk? | UpGuard
item.description: For every official MCP server, up to 15 lookalikes exist. Learn to identify these registry-layer threats and discover methods to protect your organization.

item.title: MCP: The AI Protocol Quietly Expanding Your Attack Surface | UpGuard
item.description: As AI tools adopt MCP, security teams face a new shadow IT crisis. Learn what MCP is, why these servers carry risk, and how to begin gaining visibility.

item.title: RubyGems Suspends New Signups After Hundreds of Malicious Packages Are Uploaded
item.description: RubyGems, the standard package manager for the Ruby programming language, has temporarily paused account sign ups following what has been described as a "major malicious attack." "We're dealing with a major malicious attack on RubyGems right now," Maciej Mensfeld, senior product manager for software supply chain security at Mend.io, said in a post on X. "Signups are paused for the time being.
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Mini Shai-Hulud Hits TanStack npm Packages
item.description: Mini Shai-Hulud compromises TanStack npm packages and spreads across PyPI

item.title: BWH Hotels Says Hackers Had Access to Reservation Data for 6 Months
item.description: <p>Threat actors obtained names and contact information for an unspecified number of BWH Hotels guests. </p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/bwh-hotels-says-hackers-had-access-to-reservation-data-for-6-months/">BWH Hotels Says Hackers Had Access to Reservation Data for 6 Months</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Eduard Kovacs

item.title: Hugging Face Packages Weaponized With a Single File Tweak
item.description: A tokenizer library file present in Hugging Face AI models can be manipulated to hijack the model's outputs and exfiltrate data.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Culafi

item.title: Free OnlyFans Lure Used to Spread Cross-Platform CRPx0 Malware
item.description: <p>CRPx0 is a complex, stealthy malware campaign that targets macOS and Windows systems, and appears to have Linux capabilities in development.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/free-onlyfans-lure-used-to-spread-cross-platform-crpx0-malware/">Free OnlyFans Lure Used to Spread Cross-Platform CRPx0 Malware</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Kevin Townsend

item.title: Deal Reached With Hackers to Delete Data Stolen From the Canvas Educational Platform
item.description: <p>The company that operates online learning system Canvas said it struck a deal with hackers to delete the data they pilfered in a cyberattack that created chaos for students, many of them in the middle of finals.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/deal-reached-with-hackers-to-delete-data-stolen-from-the-canvas-educational-platform/">Deal Reached With Hackers to Delete Data Stolen From the Canvas Educational Platform</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Associated Press

item.title: End‑to‑End Encrypted RCS Messaging Arrives Across iPhone and Android
item.description: Apple begins rolling out end-to-end encrypted RCS messaging between iPhone and Android in iOS 26.5

item.title: Google Says Hackers Used AI to Build Zero-Day Exploit
item.description: <p>Google says hackers used AI to help build a zero-day exploit targeting 2FA, raising concerns about AI-assisted hacking.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-google-hackers-ai-zero-day-exploit/">Google Says Hackers Used AI to Build Zero-Day Exploit</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Kezia Jungco

item.title: New TrickMo Variant Uses TON C2 and SOCKS5 to Create Android Network Pivots
item.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new version of the TrickMo Android banking trojan that uses The Open Network (TON) for command-and-control (C2). The new variant, observed by ThreatFabric between January and February 2026, has been observed actively targeting banking and cryptocurrency wallet users in France, Italy, and Austria. "TrickMo relies on a runtime-loaded APK&nbsp; (dex.module),
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: 20 Leaders Who Built the CISO Era: 2 Decades of Change
item.description: As part of Dark Reading's 20th anniversary special coverage, we profile the CISOs, founders, researchers, criminals, and policymakers who rewrote the enterprise risk playbook.
item.dc:creator: Dark Reading Editorial Team

item.title: Attackers Combine ClickFix With PySoxy Proxying to Maintain Persistence
item.description: Exploitation of open-source tools allows attackers to maintain persistent access after initial social engineering, warn ReliaQuest researchers

item.title: Webinar: What the Riskiest SOC Alerts Go Unanswered - and How Radiant Security Can Help
item.description: Why do the Riskiest SOC Alerts Go Unanswered? Security operations teams are drowning in alerts. But the real problem isn't always alert volume; it's the blind spots. The most dangerous alerts are the ones no one is investigating. A recent report from The Hacker News examined why certain high-risk alert categories - WAF, DLP, OT/IoT, dark web intelligence, and supply chain signals- consistently
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Mini Shai-Hulud Worm Compromises TanStack, Mistral AI, Guardrails AI & More Packages
item.description: TeamPCP, the threat actor behind the recentsupply chain attack spree, has been linked to the compromise of the npm and PyPI packages from TanStack, UiPath, Mistral AI, OpenSearch, and Guardrails AI as part of a fresh Mini Shai-Hulud campaign. The affected npm packages have been modified to include an obfuscated JavaScript file ("router_init.js") that's designed to profile the execution
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Worm Redux: Fresh Mini Shai-Hulud Infections Bite Supply Chain
item.description: Hundreds of npm packages infected by the self-propagating, credential-stealing worm from TeamPCP are related to the open source TanStack ecosystem.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Montalbano

item.title: Copy.Fail Linux Vulnerability
item.dc:creator: Bruce Schneier

item.title: Why Agentic AI Is Security's Next Blind Spot
item.description: Agentic AI is already running in production environments across many organizations today. It is executing tasks, consuming data, and taking actions — most likely without meaningful involvement from the security team. The industry conversation has largely framed this as a question of policy: allow it, restrict it, or monitor it? However, that framing misses the point.&nbsp; The more urgent
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Malicious Hugging Face Repository Typosquats OpenAI
item.description: HiddenLayer reveals infostealer malware in a Hugging Face repository

item.title: South Staffordshire Water Fined £1m After Data Breach
item.description: The ICO has fined South Staffordshire Water nearly £1m for a series of data protection failings

item.title: Instructure Reaches Ransom Agreement with ShinyHunters to Stop 3.65TB Canvas Leak
item.description: American educational technology company Instructure, the parent company of Canvas, said it reached an "agreement" with a decentralized cybercrime extortion group after it breached its network and threatened to leak stolen information from thousands of schools and universities. In an update shared on Monday, the Utah-based firm said it "reached an agreement with the unauthorized actor involved in
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: OpenAI Launches Daybreak for AI-Powered Vulnerability Detection and Patch Validation
item.description: OpenAI has launched Daybreak, a new cybersecurity initiative that brings together frontier artificial intelligence (AI) model capabilities and Codex Security to help organizations identify and patch vulnerabilities before attackers find a way in using the same issues. "Daybreak combines the intelligence of OpenAI models, the extensibility of Codex as an agentic harness, and our partners across
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: iOS 26.5 Brings Default End-to-End Encrypted RCS Messaging Between iPhone and Android
item.description: Apple on Monday officially released iOS 26.5 with support for end-to-end encryption (E2EE) to Rich Communication Services (RCS) in beta as part of a "cross-industry effort" to replace traditional SMS with a more secure alternative. To that end, E2EE RCS messaging is rolling out to iPhone users running iOS 26.5 with supported carriers and Android users on the latest version of Google Messages.
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: FCC Softens Ban on Foreign-Made Routers
item.description: The Federal Communications Commission eased some restrictions and pushed back deadlines for foreign router manufacturers, but the ban is still in place.
item.dc:creator: Jai Vijayan

item.title: Tech Can't Stop These Threats — Your People Can
item.description: Security controls can do only so much. Here are four attacks where your employees are usually your first, and only, line of cyber defense.
item.dc:creator: A. Stryker

item.title: FCC Robocall Crackdown Raises Privacy Concerns Over Mandatory ID Checks
item.description: <p>The FCC’s proposed robocall crackdown could force carriers to verify customer identities, raising privacy concerns over anonymous phone use.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-fcc-robocall-crackdown-privacy-id-checks/">FCC Robocall Crackdown Raises Privacy Concerns Over Mandatory ID Checks</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Aminu Abdullahi

item.title: TeamPCP Compromises Checkmarx Jenkins AST Plugin Weeks After KICS Supply Chain Attack
item.description: Checkmarx has confirmed that a modified version of the Jenkins AST plugin was published to the Jenkins Marketplace. "If you are using Checkmarx Jenkins AST plugin, you need to ensure that you are using the version 2.0.13-829.vc72453fa_1c16 that was published on December 17, 2025 or previously," the cybersecurity company said in a statement over the weekend. As of writing, Checkmarx has released
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Mac Users Warned Over Fake Claude Install Instructions
item.description: <p>Hackers are using Google Ads and Claude shared chats to target Mac users with fake setup instructions that can install malware.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-claude-mac-download-google-ads-malware/">Mac Users Warned Over Fake Claude Install Instructions</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Liz Ticong

item.title: cPanel CVE-2026-41940 Under Active Exploitation to Deploy Filemanager Backdoor
item.description: A threat actor named Mr_Rot13 has been attributed to the exploitation of a recently disclosed critical cPanel flaw to deploy a backdoor codenamed Filemanager on compromised environments. The attack exploits CVE-2026-41940, a vulnerability impacting cPanel and WebHost Manager (WHM) that could result in an authentication bypass and allow remote attackers to gain elevated control of the control
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: 1.8 Billion Gmail Users May Want to Check This AI Privacy Setting
item.description: <p>Google’s new Gmail AI personalization features are raising privacy concerns. Here’s what users should know and how to review smart settings.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-google-gmail-ai-personalization-privacy-settings/">1.8 Billion Gmail Users May Want to Check This AI Privacy Setting</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Esther Shein

item.title: Spotlight On: Worldline, a New Principal Participating Organization
item.dc:creator: amalone@pcisecuritystandards.org (Alicia Malone)

item.title: Hackers Used AI to Develop First Known Zero-Day 2FA Bypass for Mass Exploitation
item.description: Google on Monday disclosed that it identified an unknown threat actor using a zero-day exploit that it said was likely developed with an artificial intelligence (AI) system, marking the first time the technology has been put to use in the wild in a malicious context for vulnerability discovery and exploit generation. The activity is said to be the work of cybercrime threat actors who appear to
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: TrickMo Variant Routes Android Trojan Traffic Through TON
item.description: ThreatFabric finds new TrickMo Android banking trojan variant routing C2 through The Open Network

item.title: 'Dirty Frag' Exploit Poised to Blow Up on Enterprise Linux Distros
item.description: The privilege escalation vulnerability, which is similar to other Linux flaws like Copy Fail and Dirty Pipe, may already be under limited exploitation.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Montalbano

item.title: Rushed Patches Follow Broken Embargo on New Linux Kernel Vulnerabilities
item.description: Two new high-severity vulnerabilities, dubbed ’Dirty Frag’ when chained, have been found in the Linux kernel, affecting most Linux distributions

item.title: Fake Claude Code Page Pushes PowerShell Stealer at Devs
item.description: Ontinue uncovers fake Claude Code installer pushing PowerShell stealer abusing Chrome's IElevator2

item.title: Hackers Use AI for Exploit Development, Attack Automation
item.description: Cyber adversaries have long used AI, but now attackers are using large language models to develop exploits and orchestrate complex attacks.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Culafi

item.title: Hackers Observed Using AI to Develop Zero-Day for the First Time
item.description: Google Threat Intelligence Group details how cybercriminals attempted to launch a campaign based around an AI-developed Zero-Day targeting open-source software

item.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Linux Rootkit, macOS Crypto Stealer, WebSocket Skimmers and More
item.description: Rough Monday. Somebody poisoned a trusted download again, somebody else turned cloud servers into public housing, and a few crews are still getting into boxes with bugs that should’ve died years ago — the same old holes, same lazy access paths, same “how the hell is this still open” feeling. One report this week basically reads like a guy tripped over root access by accident and decided to stay
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Cyber Espionage Group Targets Aviation Firms to Steal Map Data
item.description: The campaign quietly compromises aerospace and drone operators to exfiltrate GIS files, terrain models, and GPS data and gain a clear picture of adversaries' world view.
item.dc:creator: Robert Lemos

item.title: Your Purple Team Isn't Purple — It's Just Red and Blue in the Same Room
item.description: Defending a network at 2 am looks a lot like this: an analyst copy-pasting a hash from a PDF into a SIEM query. A red team script is being rewritten by hand so the blue team can use it. A patch waiting on a change-approval window that's longer than the exploitation window itself. Nobody in that chain is incompetent. Every human is doing their job correctly. The problem is the system, its
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: LLMs and Text-in-Text Steganography
item.description: <p>Turns out that LLMs are <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.20075">really good</a> at hiding text messages in other text messages.</p>
item.dc:creator: Bruce Schneier

item.title: US: FCC Relaxes Foreign-Made Router Ban to Allow for Security Updates
item.description: The same extension applies to security updates shipped to US-based users of foreign-made drones

item.title: ShinyHunters Escalates Canvas Extortion with School by School Ransom Campaign
item.description: ShinyHunters has escalated its Canvas extortion campaign, defacing hundreds of school login pages and threatening to leak stolen data unless institutions negotiate

item.title: Zara Data Breach Impacts Nearly 200,000 Customers
item.description: ShinyHunters gets away with emails and other data on 200,000 Zara customers

item.title: Eyes wide open: How to mitigate the security and privacy risks of smart glasses
item.description: Smart glasses allow anyone to track and record the world around them. That could put your data and the privacy of those nearby at risk.

item.title: Police Shut Relaunched Crimenetwork Dark Web Marketplace
item.description: Spanish police have arrested the suspected administrator of German dark web marketplace Crimenetwork

item.title: Fake OpenAI Privacy Filter Repo Hits #1 on Hugging Face, Draws 244K Downloads
item.description: A malicious Hugging Face repository managed to take a spot in the platform's trending list by impersonating OpenAI's Privacy Filter open-weight model to deliver a Rust-based information stealer to Windows users. The project, named Open-OSS/privacy-filter, masqueraded as its legitimate counterpart released by OpenAI late last month (openai/privacy-filter), including copying the entire description
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Ollama Out-of-Bounds Read Vulnerability Allows Remote Process Memory Leak
item.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed a critical security vulnerability in Ollama that, if successfully exploited, could allow a remote, unauthenticated attacker to leak its entire process memory. The out-of-bounds read flaw, which likely impacts over 300,000 servers globally, is tracked as CVE-2026-7482 (CVSS score: 9.1). It has been codenamed&nbsp;Bleeding Llama by Cyera. Ollama is a
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: cPanel, WHM Release Fixes for Three New Vulnerabilities — Patch Now
item.description:
cPanel has released updates to address three vulnerabilities in cPanel and Web Host Manager (WHM) that could be exploited to achieve privilege escalation, code execution, and denial-of-service. The list of vulnerabilities is as follows -
CVE-2026-29201 (CVSS score: 4.3) - An insufficient input validation of the feature file name in the "feature::LOADFEATUREFILE" adminbin call that could result
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: What is a Proxy Server? How They Work + Security Risks | UpGuard
item.description: A proxy server is an intermediary server that retrieves data from an Internet source on behalf of a user. Learn more about how they work and their uses.

item.title: ShinyHunters Extorts Universities in New Instructure Canvas Hack
item.description: <p>ShinyHunters-linked attackers defaced Canvas portals, disrupting finals week access and exposing SaaS security risks for schools.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-shinyhunters-canvas-portal-defacement/">ShinyHunters Extorts Universities in New Instructure Canvas Hack</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Ken Underhill

item.title: Friday Squid Blogging: Giant Squid Live in the Waters of Western Australia
item.dc:creator: Bruce Schneier

item.title: AWS Rex Is a Big Step for Agentic AI Security, But Not the Final Layer
item.description: <p>AWS Rex adds runtime guardrails for agentic AI, but security leaders still need data-layer controls to satisfy compliance and audit demands.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-aws-rex-agentic-ai-data-security-gap/">AWS Rex Is a Big Step for Agentic AI Security, But Not the Final Layer</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Tim Freestone

item.title: ShinyHunters Claims Second Attack Against Instructure
item.description: The edtech company is struggling to wrest control from its hackers. PII belonging to hundreds of millions of people is on the line.
item.dc:creator: Nate Nelson

item.title: Google Play Scam Apps Hit 7.3M Downloads with Fake Call Logs
item.description: <p>ESET found 28 CallPhantom scam apps on Google Play that promised fake call logs and had reached more than 7.3 million downloads before being removed.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-callphantom-google-play-scam/">Google Play Scam Apps Hit 7.3M Downloads with Fake Call Logs</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Liz Ticong

item.title: TCLBANKER Banking Trojan Targets Financial Platforms via WhatsApp and Outlook Worms
item.description: Threat hunters have flagged a previously undocumented Brazilian banking trojan dubbed TCLBANKER that's capable of targeting 59 banking, fintech, and cryptocurrency platforms. The activity is being tracked by Elastic Security Labs under the moniker REF3076. The malware family is assessed to be a major update of the Maverick&nbsp;family, which is known to leverage a worm called SORVEPOTEL to
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Insider Betting on Polymarket
item.dc:creator: Bruce Schneier

item.title: Millions of Windows PCs Face a Secure Boot Update Deadline in 2026
item.description: <p>Microsoft Secure Boot certificates from 2011 begin expiring in June 2026. Here’s how to check whether your Windows PC has the 2023 update.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-windows-secure-boot-certificates-2026/">Millions of Windows PCs Face a Secure Boot Update Deadline in 2026</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Joseph Ofonagoro

item.title: Fake Call History Apps Stole Payments From Users After 7.3 Million Play Store Downloads
item.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered fraudulent apps on the official Google Play Store for Android that falsely claimed to offer access to call histories for any phone number, only to trick users into joining a subscription that provided fake data and incurred financial loss. The 28 apps have collectively racked up more than 7.3 million downloads, with one of them alone accounting for over
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: One Click, Total Shutdown: The "Patient Zero" Webinar on Killing Stealth Breaches
item.description: The hardest part of cybersecurity isn't the technology, it’s the people. Every major breach you’ve read about lately usually starts the same way: one employee, one clever email, and one "Patient Zero" infection. In 2026, hackers are using AI to make these "first clicks" nearly impossible to spot. If a single laptop gets compromised on your watch, do you have a plan to stop it from taking down
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Quasar Linux RAT Steals Developer Credentials for Software Supply Chain Compromise
item.description: A previously undocumented Linux implant codenamed Quasar Linux RAT (QLNX) is targeting developers' systems to establish a silent foothold as well as facilitate a broad range of post-compromise functionality, such as credential harvesting, keylogging, file manipulation, clipboard monitoring, and network tunneling. "QLNX targets developers and DevOps credentials across the software supply chain,"
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Australian Cyber Security Centre Issues Alert Over ClickFix Attacks
item.description: ACSC warns over a campaign targeting organizations which uses ClickFix to deliver Vidar infostealer malware

item.title: Shadow IT: Tiering the Unseen to Manage Vendor Risk | UpGuard
item.description: Invisible apps become riskier with time. Use real-time user signals to categorize every vendor in your ecosystem based on its true risk profile.

item.title: One Missed Threat Per Week: What 25M Alerts Reveal About Low-Severity Risk
item.description: The dark secret of enterprise security operations is that defenders have quietly institutionalized the practice of not looking. This is not just anecdotal, but rather backed by a recent report investigating more than 25 million security alerts, including informational and low-severity, across live enterprise environments.&nbsp; The dataset behind these findings includes 10 million monitored
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: PCPJack Campaign Boots TeamPCP Off Compromised Machines
item.description: SentinelOne believes the PCPJack campaign may be the brainchild of a former TeamPCP member

item.title: New Linux PamDOORa Backdoor Uses PAM Modules to Steal SSH Credentials
item.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a new Linux backdoor named PamDOORa that's being advertised on the Rehub Russian cybercrime forum for $1,600 by a threat actor called "darkworm." The backdoor is designed as a Pluggable Authentication Module (PAM)-based post-exploitation toolkit that enables persistent SSH access by means of a magic password and specific TCP port combination.
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Linux Kernel Dirty Frag LPE Exploit Enables Root Access Across Major Distributions
item.description: Details have emerged about a new, unpatched local privilege escalation (LPE) vulnerability impacting the Linux kernel. Dubbed Dirty Frag, it has been described as a successor to Copy Fail (CVE-2026-31431, CVSS score: 7.8), a recently disclosed LPE flaw impacting the Linux kernel that has since come under active exploitation in the wild. The vulnerability was reported to Linux kernel maintainers
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
item.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.
item.dc:creator: BrianKrebs

item.title: The Shadow Supply Chain: A 72.9% Gap In Your Vendor Inventory | UpGuard
item.description: Your real vendor inventory is 8x larger than your official list. Discover how user behavior creates a hidden shadow supply chain that legacy tools miss.

item.title: After Replacing TeamPCP Malware, 'PCPJack' Steals Cloud Secrets
item.description: PCPJack makes innovative use of parquet files for stealthy, pre-validated target discovery as it canvasses multiple cloud environments.
item.dc:creator: Nate Nelson

item.title: Has CISA Finally Found Its New Leader in Tom Parker?
item.description: Dark Reading investigates rumors that Tom Parker, a board room &quot;operator&quot; and longtime cyber exec, could be next in line to take over CISA.
item.dc:creator: Becky Bracken

item.title: Ivanti EPMM CVE-2026-6973 RCE Under Active Exploitation Grants Admin-Level Access
item.description: Ivanti is warning that a new security flaw impacting Endpoint Manager Mobile (EPMM) has been explored in limited attacks in the wild. The high-severity vulnerability, CVE-2026-6973 (CVSS score: 7.2), is a case of improper input validation affecting EPMM before versions 12.6.1.1, 12.7.0.1, and 12.8.0.1. It allows "a remotely authenticated user with administrative access to achieve remote code
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: PCPJack Credential Stealer Exploits 5 CVEs to Spread Worm-Like Across Cloud Systems
item.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a new credential theft framework dubbed PCPJack that targets exposed cloud infrastructure and ousts any artifacts linked to TeamPCP from the environments. "The toolset harvests credentials from cloud, container, developer, productivity, and financial services, then exfiltrates the data through attacker-controlled infrastructure while attempting
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Legacy Security Tools Failing Data Protection, Capital One Software Report Finds
item.description: Traditional network security tools are undermining data protection, with Forrester and Capital One Software research warning AI adoption is impossible without rethinking data security

item.title: Cline Kanban Flaw Lets Websites Hijack AI Coding Agents
item.description: Oasis Security finds critical Cline kanban WebSocket flaw exposing AI coding agents to hijack

item.title: OpenAI and Anthropic LLMs Used in Critical Infrastructure Cyber-Attack, Warns Dragos
item.description: Commercial AI models were used to help plan and conduct cyber-attack against operational technology of a water and drainage facility, say researchers

item.title: PAN-OS RCE Exploit Under Active Use Enabling Root Access and Espionage
item.description: Palo Alto Networks has disclosed that threat actors may have attempted to unsuccessfully exploit a recently disclosed critical security flaw as early as April 9, 2026. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2026-0300 (CVSS score: 9.3/8.7), a buffer overflow vulnerability in the User-ID Authentication Portal service of Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS software that could allow an unauthenticated attacker
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Fake Claude AI Site Drops Beagle Backdoor on Windows Users
item.description: Sophos finds fake Claude site spreading DonutLoader and a new Beagle backdoor via DLL sideloading

item.title: AI-Driven Cyberattack on Mexico Couldn't Breach OT Systems
item.description: The most sophisticated AI-integrated campaign to date hit a brick wall in the form of a SCADA login screen.
item.dc:creator: Nate Nelson

item.title: 'TrustFall' Convention Exposes Claude Code Execution Risk
item.description: Malicious repositories can trigger code execution in Claude Code, Cursor CLI, Gemini CLI, and CoPilot CLI with minimal or no user interaction, thanks to skimpy warning dialogs.
item.dc:creator: Jai Vijayan

item.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Edge Plaintext Passwords, ICS 0-Days, Patch-or-Die Alerts and 25+ New Stories
item.description: Bad week. Turns out the easiest way to get hacked in 2026 is still the same old garbage: shady packages, fake apps, forgotten DNS junk, scam ads, and stolen logins getting dumped into Discord channels like it’s normal. Some of these attack chains don’t even feel sophisticated anymore. More like some tired guy with a Telegram account and too much free time. The worst part is how often this stuff
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Smart Glasses for the Authorities
item.description: <p>ICE is <a href="https://www.kenklippenstein.com/p/exclusive-ice-glasses">developing</a> its own version of smart glasses, with facial recognition tied to various databases.</p>
item.dc:creator: Bruce Schneier

item.title: Day Zero Readiness: The Operational Gaps That Break Incident Response
item.description: Having an incident response retainer, or even a pre-approved external incident response firm, is not the same as being ready for an incident. A retainer means someone will answer the phone. Operational readiness determines whether that team can do meaningful work the moment they do.&nbsp; That distinction matters far more than many organizations realize. In the first hours of a security incident
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Daemon Tools Developer Confirms Software Was Trojanized
item.description: A China-linked threat actor backdoored a version of Daemon Tools to infect thousands
